text: The patient is positioned prone with supported chest and buttocks. A single lumen endotracheal tube is used for two-lung anesthesia. A Veress needle creates pneumothorax for partial lung collapse. A trocar is introduced at the angle of the seventh rib, with additional trocars placed in the fifth and ninth intercostal spaces for triangulation. The lung is visibly collapsed, eliminating the need for traction.

The surgeon incises the mediastinal pleura both dorsally and ventrally over the tumor for en bloc removal. The azygos vein is visualized, and the mediastinal pleura is incised on either end. After skeletonization, silk is used for double ligature on either side, with the first ligature placed dorsally due to blood flow direction and the second ventrally. If using a harmonic ACE, a single ligature is sufficient.

The surgeon divides the vein and mobilizes the esophagus circumferentially at the level of the normal esophagus. A hook is used to coagulate perforators during mobilization. Umbilical tape is placed across the esophagus for traction, with the tumor site visualized and dissection performed on the left side, as well as dorsal and ventral aspects.

The surgeon achieves adequate exposure of the ventral bronchus, clearly visualizing nodes extending in continuity on the left side of the esophagus. Clips are applied to divide these nodes and lymphatic chains to facilitate their removal. The dissection of the esophagus continues towards the neck thoracic inlet, with vessels being divided using a hook or a 5mm curved harmonic scalpel, taking the tissue en bloc.

The surgeon performs a vagetomy, carefully manipulating the esophagus and surrounding soft tissues to protect the membranous portion of the trachea. The esophagus is lifted away from the trachea using two-hand manipulation to avoid membrane injury. Dissection extends up to the inferior diaphragm, allowing removal of nodes along the right lacrimal nerve and caudally up to the crura of the diaphragm. Once the esophagus is fully mobilized, it is lifted by an assistant to expose the mediastinal area for nodal clearance.

The surgeon begins dissection from the pericardium upwards to the carina, removing nodal tissues in one block. Clips are carefully removed from the left bronchus, completing the infracarinal nodal dissection. The tracheal bifurcation and both bronchi are clearly visualized. The procedure continues with supracarinal node dissection at the level of the aorta, where nodes are picked up and the right lacrymogen is visualized. The dissection progresses towards the thoracic inlet.
After completing nodal clearance and ensuring the esophagus is free, the surgeon checks for hemostasis before changing the patient's position to prone.

The patient is positioned supine for gastric mobilization using a five-port technique. The surgeon incises the lesser omentum and divides the left gastric vein with a harmonic scalpel, ensuring safety by clipping the vein before division. Nodes are dissected from the celiac and right common hepatic regions. The left gastric artery is divided, and dissection continues from the upper border of the pancreas towards the spleen's hilum, removing nodes along the splenic artery and soft tissues up to the diaphragm.

The surgeon mobilizes the lower end of the esophagus by dividing the renal esophageal membrane. The fundus is released from the diaphragm, and the pancreas is rotated downward to clear nodes along the celiac axis. The dissection proceeds towards the gastrocolic omentum, dividing veins between the colon and the greater curvature while protecting the gastroepiploic pedicle. The division continues until all short gastric vessels are divided, freeing the fundus completely. The hiatus is visualized, and the gastrocolic momentum is divided up to the second part, head of the pancreas.

The surgeon creates a tension-free mobilization of the stomach, making a transverse incision in the neck to identify and divide the esophagus. A rail tube is fixed to the lower end of the esophagus and pushed into the mediastinum. The esophagus, along with the rail tube, is pulled into the peritoneal cavity, where the tumor is visualized. An incision is made extending from one of the ports over the antrum, and the esophagus, rail tube, and stomach are exteriorized. The stomach is resected along the lesser curvature up to the fundus to form a gastric tube with a diameter of five to six centimeters. The gastric tube is pulled up into the posterior mediastinum using the rail tube, and a running stitch is made over the staple line. Pyloromyotomy is performed through the same incision.

The surgeon checks for gastric twist and narrows the hiatus, fixing the stomach to the hiatus crura with stitches to prevent herniation. A tube is placed, and the duodenal jejunal flexure is identified, followed by the creation of a jejunostomy to facilitate esophagus removal and nodal clearance.
